coredata.dc:title: A straight forward synthetic route for solid-emissive carbon dots from AIE-active precursors
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Shumei
Yue, Zhenxin
Li, Min
Wang, Guan
Xu, Ziqiang
Wang, Shimin
Gu, Xinggui
Wang, Erjing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with solid-state emission are becoming the eager pursuit for researchers under the continuous and in-depth development of carbon materials. A general strategy for efficiently constructing solid-emissive CDs is of importance. Here, solid-emissive CDs were successfully prepared using aggregation-induced emission (AIE) amino-substituted tetraphenylethylene as precursor. Thus obtained CDs well inherited the AIE properties of precursors with improved pH stability and bio-imaging performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Tetraphenylethylene
Carbon dots
Stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(Top) Schematic route to AIE-active CDs-NH2 from precursor TPE-NH2. (Bottom) (A) PL spectra of CDs-NH2 in DMSO/H2O mixtures with different H2O fractions (f
w ). (B) Plot of relative PL intensity (I/I 0) of CDs-NH2 versus the composition of the DMSO/H2O solution (Inset: fluorescence image of solid CDs-NH2 under irradiation with a hand-held UV lamp). Concentration 4 μg/mL, excitation wavelength: 323 nm.

. (A) TEM image of powder CDs-NH2 with scale bar 100 nm. Inset was the HR-TEM image (scale bar: 2 nm), which showed crystallized graphite inner core with lattice spacing 0.315 nm. (B) Particle size distribution of CDs-NH2 calculated according to TEM image. (C–E) High-resolution XPS C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra of CDs-NH2, respectively.

(Top) Pictures of patterned CDs-NH2 written TLC silica gel plate before and after fumigation in trifluoroacetic acid and triethylamine, taken under daylight and 365 nm UV irradiation, respectively. (Bottom) (A) PL spectra of CDs-NH2 in different pH buffer solutions, and (B) plot of relative PL intensity (I/I
0 ) versus pH value of solution. I
0 = emission intensity in a buffer with pH = 7.

Images of HepG2 cells after incubation with CDs-NH2 (15 μg/mL) for 24 h under (A) bright field and (B) photo-excitation by fluorescence microscopy. (C) Merged images of the corresponding bright-field and fluorescence images. Excitation wavelength: 330–385 nm. Fluorescence collected at 449–520 nm. Scale bar: 50 μ m.

head.ce:title: A straight forward synthetic route for solid-emissive carbon dots from AIE-active precursors

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with solid-state emission are becoming the eager pursuit for researchers under the continuous and in-depth development of carbon materials. A general strategy for efficiently constructing solid-emissive CDs is of importance. Here, solid-emissive CDs were successfully prepared using aggregation-induced emission (AIE) amino-substituted tetraphenylethylene as precursor. Thus obtained CDs well inherited the AIE properties of precursors with improved pH stability and bio-imaging performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to outstanding optoelectronic properties, carbon dots (CDs) as a rising star of carbon materials, have attracted intensive attentions in luminescence, energy conversion, biomedicine, micro-/nano-devices, etc. [1]. The prominent merit of CDs is their high-efficiency fluorescence in solution state, although an unavoidable fact we have to face is that the emission of CDs is intricate and the mechanism behind has not been elaborated and clarified. It is generally regarded that there exist several sources for CDs fluorescence: surface defect-related traps, quantum size effects, intermediate fluorophores crosslinked with formed polymer chains and intrinsic sp2 carbon core in CDs [2]. However, the destroy of balanced surface states and formation of energy/electron transfer upon aggregation usually bring about fluorescence quenching, challenging the practical applications of CDs [3,4]. Compared with CDs emissive in solution, the study of solid-state-emissive CDs is seriously lagged due to the poor understanding on fluorescence mechanism of CDs.

In order to afford solid-state luminescence, CDs were usually dispersed in rigid polymers [5–7], inorganics [8], starch [9], etc. These solid matrices, just like solvents, dilute CDs particles so as to realize emission retention. However, the usage of solid matrices cannot eliminate aggregation-causing quenching basically [10]. Additionally, a solid matrix could also be added or in-situ formed during CDs preparation. For example, in the microwave-assisted hydrothermal procedure, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH-792) was added as distance barrier chains, and the formed CDs had high fluorescence quantum yield in the solid matrix, featuring solid emission characteristic [11]. In the presence of CDs, the gelation of tetraethyl orthosilicate was triggered, and the resultant CDs-silica composite xerogel exhibited high solid-state emission (> 40%) [12]. The application of CaCl2 could result in the formation of inflated foam under vacuum heating, thus the following dehydration and carbonization of citric acid and urea precursors occurred in such narrow and ultra-thin space of the foam wall so as to produce size-uniform isolated CDs. These CDs exhibited strong luminescence in both dispersed and aggregated states [13]. Yang et al. synthesized hydrophobic CDs with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission. The formation of hydrophobic CDs clusters induces the turning-off of the blue emission, as the carbonized cores suffer from π-π stacking interactions, and the turning-on of the red ﬂuorescence, due to restriction of the surfaces’ intramolecular rotation around disulﬁde bonds, realizing solid-state emission [14]. In all, it could be deducted that the selection of precursors and preparation routes are occasional, and a universal guideline or route to solid-emissive CDs is still in deficiency.

On the other hand, a memory effect often takes actions in CDs, that is to say, the residues, groups, and properties of precursors are preserved in the formed CDs. This character provides clues to CDs preparation for intended purpose [15]. For instance, L-cysteine residue appeared on the surface of CDs synthesized from L-cysteine for selective Golgi targeting [16]. The antibacterial activity of ciprofloxacin hydrochloride was preserved on derived CDs for broad spectrum antimicrobic study [17]. The room-temperature phosphorescence of trimellitic acid precursor descended to thereafter CDs with some differences [18]. These studies spur us to speculate whether we could take reference from the famous aggregation-induced emission (AIE) strategy to construct solid emissive CDs, just using AIE precursors, as long as the AIE merits of precursors are inherited [19]? The rotors in AIE molecules substantially take account for the AIE property according to RIM mechanism [20]. It is reasonable that the inert rotors existing in AIE precursors would be much likely preserved in derived CDs and descend their AIE property to the CDs simultaneously, or even improve luminescence performance. Furthermore, various existing electron-rich groups such as amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aromatic rings are favorable to the solid emission via rotation inhibition and clusteroluminescence process [21] and would also contribute to the solid emission of CDs.

As a proof-of-concept, we carried out solvothermal treatment of AIE-active tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and amino-substituted tetraphenylethylene (TPE-NH2) in water, ethanol, or acetic acid, in the hope of preparing AIE-active CDs. Only the usage of acetic acid could induce the formation of CDs from TPE-NH2 (CDs-NH2) route while no CDs were formed in the TPE route, no matter what solvent or temperature was adopted, indicating the necessity of heteroatoms for solid emission [22]. CDs-NH2 was almost non-emissive in DMSO, but the photoluminescence became incrementally increased when dispersed in DMSO/H2O mixture with increasing volume fractions of H2O (f
w ), attributing to typical AIE property, thanks to the AIE precursor ( Fig. 1). Surprisingly, compared with the precursor, the CDs had superior stability under different pH conditions, and the biological tests in HepG2 cells indicated stable and brilliant blue emission imaging.

TPE-NH2 was synthesized according to literature [23]. The solvents for optical spectroscopy measurement were treated using standard methodology. Other solvents, including dichloromethane, methanol, trifluoroacetic acid, petroleum ether, and acetic acid were of high analytical purity and used as received.

1H and 13C NMR spectra were measured on an Inova 400 MHz spectrometer using DMSO-d 6 as solvent and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal standard. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were taken on a Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Both the excitation and emission slit width was set at 5 nm. The fluorescence quantum yields were measured on an Edinburgh FLS 920 spectrometer using integrating sphere technique. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using an X-ray diffractometer (Akishima-shi, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The elemental binding energy information was analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi). The Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet-iS50). The Raman spectrum was performed by confocal Raman microscopy (Raman-LabRAM HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Tunneling electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was performed on Titan G2 60–300 FEI. For sample preparation, solid CDs were dispersed in ethanol, and then the solution was drop-casted on ultrathin carbon film for drying naturally. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments and zeta potentials were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle size/zeta potential analyzer. The fluorescence images were obtained by photographing using a domestic mobile phone. The pH buffer solutions were prepared with hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, citric acid/sodium citrate, disodium hydrogen phosphate/sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and sodium carbonate/sodium bicarbonate systems. Liver HepG2 cells were cultured in the DMEM medium in a 5% CO2 humidity incubator at 37 °C. A Nikon laser scanning confocal microscope was used to observe the cell imaging.

Synthesis of CDs-NH
2 CDs-NH2 was synthesized using TPE-NH2 as the precursor through solvothermal process. TPE-NH2 (0.1426 g) was dissolved in 60 mL acetic acid. Then the mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reactor was automatically cooled down to room temperature. A large amount of water was added for diluting the solution. The mixture was extracted with CH2Cl2 (40 mL×3), dried with anhydrous MgSO4, and dried with a rotary evaporator. The residue was purified through silica column chromatography using CH2Cl2 and methanol for gradient elution to afford a light yellow powder as the target product 0.022 g. Yield: 15%.

Firstly, the morphology of the CDs-NH2 was measured by TEM and HR-TEM. Prepared CDs-NH2 was mono-dispersed particles with an average size of 6.8 nm ( Fig. 2A-B). In the HR-TEM image, the clear lattice spacing of 0.315 nm corresponded to the graphitic (002) facet of carbon atoms, indicating the successful preparation of carbon nanomaterials. A series of diffraction peaks in the XRD spectra demonstrated the good crystallinity of the as-prepared CDs (see Electronic Supplementary Information, Fig. S1). In general, CDs show a broad diffraction peak in XRD spectrum, indicating amorphous structure. The acquisition of the good-crystallinity graphite structure in our CDs may be related with the purification of CDs using column chromatography. Several literatures also reported high-crystallinity CDs with obvious and sharp diffraction peaks [24]. The presence of phenyl rings in precursor may be helpful for the formation of crystalline graphite segments in CDs [25]. The graphitic structure of thus obtained carbon nanomaterial was also confirmed by the Raman spectrum (Fig. S2) with D and G bands at 1335 cm–1 and 1599 cm–1, respectively. Secondly, in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S3), the strong absorption bands at 3435, 1667, 1603 and 1402 cm–1 were related to amide groups, assigned to the (CO)–N–H stretching vibration, CO stretching vibration, N-H in-plane bending vibration and C-N stretching vibration, respectively, verifying that dehydration-condensation reaction between TPE-NH2 and acetic acid occurred in the solvothermal process [13]. The peaks at 1511 and 1492 cm–1, characteristic of the aromatic CC stretching vibration, appeared both in spectra of precursor and CDs, suggesting the existence of phenyl rotors in the formed CDs. Additionally, the stretching vibrations of –CN and C–N–C at 1538 and 1317 cm–1 were also detected, revealing amidation and carbonization occurred for CDs formation. Furthermore, the bending vibrational peak of NH2 at 1618 cm–1 was greatly weakened, which means that the amino NH2 largely turned into amido N and participated into formation of heterocyclic structures.

In the XPS full spectra, three peaks were located at 284.8 eV, 400.7 eV, and 532.6 eV, attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, and the atomic ratios were calculated to be 89.10%, 7.27% and 3.62% (Fig. S4). Three obvious peaks existed in the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C), corresponding to CC/C–C (284.1 eV), C–N (285.1 eV), and CN/CO (291.5 eV) functional groups. The N 1 s band was deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.8 eV, assigned to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [14], further verifying the incorporation of NH2 of TPE-NH2 into heterocyclic structures (Fig. 2D). Another evidence for nitrogen’s aromatization was that the chemical shift peak at 5.05 ppm (ascribed to –NH2 of TPE-NH2) became hardly detectable in 1HNMR of CDs-NH2 (Fig S5). Oxygen atoms were presented in the form of CO (531.9 eV) and C–O (532.9 eV) from O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2E), indicative of participation of acetic acid into the carbonization.15 Phenolic hydroxyl and aldehyde hydrogen groups appeared in the CDs structure according to the 1H NMR spectrum. There were four groups of absorption peaks between chemical shift 7.33 ppm and 6.84 ppm (Fig. S5), ascribed to hydrogen atoms on aromatic ring. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig S6), signals from chemical shift of 145–115 ppm are associated with sp
2 carbon atoms and signals in the range of 175–165 ppm correspond to carbonyl groups. All in all, multiple groups formed in the CDs such as –OH, –COOH, –COOR, phenyl, pyridinyl, and pyrrolyl. The existence of cyclized structures, carboxyl, and hydroxyl, especially the hydrophobic aromatic rotors, is essential to the AIE properties, because the motions of these groups would be hindered in aggregated CDs states.

The maximum absorption peaks of solid CDs-NH2 were located at 255 nm and 319 nm in Fig. S7A. The former peak was ascribed to the π-π * transition while the latter was from the n-π * transition. The maxima peak positions did not show obvious shift when CDs were dissolved with different solvents (Fig. S7B). Even in acetic acid solvent, the absorption character did not show any obvious change, indicating the stable structure of the CDs, which would be further confirmed by following pH stability test. CDs-NH2 did not show notable fluorescence when dissolved in solvents including THF, CH2Cl2, CH3CN, 1,4-dioxane, ethyl acetate, N,N-dimethylformamide and DMSO, which differed from reported results [5], while strong blue light emitted as solid powder. The non-fluorescent CDs-NH2 in DMSO solution became highly emissive when the solvent changed to a DMSO/H2O mixture with water faction above 70 vol% (H2O is a poor solvent for CDs-NH2). The PL intensity at water faction 99% was 72 times that in pure DMSO solution (Fig. 1A-B). The emission peaks were located at 460 nm when water fractions were 70% and 80% and 486 nm for water fractions of 90%, 95%, and 99%. This was reasonable. High H2O fraction will decrease the solubility of CD-NH2 and induce nano-aggregation of CD-NH2. In the aggregated state, the free rotations of surface groups including phenyl ring derived from TPE-NH2 precursor were inhibited so as to trigger the radiative decay of excitation energy, thus showing AIE behavior. Dynamic light scattering test also indicated the correlation of emission enhancement with CDs aggregation (Fig S8). The solid powder of CDs-NH2 has a photoluminescence quantum yield of 34.7%, peaked at 460 nm. In addition, the emission peak was un-shifted when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 410 nm (Fig. S9), indicating the sole and stable emissive sources [26]. These results demonstrated that the AIE optical properties of TPE derivatives had been well retained after thermal treatment of AIE precursors for CDs conversion. Nevertheless the emission peak at aggregated state was a little red shift (from 500 nm of TPE-NH2 to 486 nm of CDs-NH2 in DMSO/H2O (1:99)), probably due to the different intramolecular electron pull-push effects [27].

For application at different occasions, the pH stability of CDs was measured ( Fig. 3). First, a THF solution (4 μg/mL) of CDs-NH2 was used as fluorescent ink for pattern writing and a THF solution of TPE-NH2 was also prepared for comparison. A “C-NH2” pattern was spelled out on a silica gel strip using the fluorescent ink, which was emissive under UV irradiation. The blue emissive pattern still was observable five minutes after the silica gel strip was fumigated in trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) or triethylamine (TEA) atmosphere, and at the same time no new emission was observed. In contrary, the blue emissive pattern spelled out with TPE-NH2 precursor was totally invisible after fumigation in trifluoroacetic acid atmosphere and only emissive in triethylamine environment (Fig. S10). It indicated that CDs-NH2 had better pH stability than TPE-NH2 regarding to emission. The PL intensity and emission peak of CDs-NH2 solution (DMSO:H2O = 5:95 v/v) under pH 1–14 were generally kept constant while the PL intensity of TPE-NH2 was abruptly decreased below pH = 4 due to formation of protonated species. DLS tests in DMSO/H2O (10:90 v/v) and THF/H2O (10:90 v/v) with different pH were also carried out. As seen in Fig. S11-12, nanoaggregates of CDs-NH2 were all formed (several hundred nanometers in size) under basic, neutral, and acidic conditions, although with zeta potentials difference (Table S1). It indicated that the emitting groups were not influenced by the acidity or basicity of the aqueous phase. For comparison, TPE was also measured under the same condition (Fig. S12 -13) and nanoaggregates also formed, affording an AIE behavior. The high-intensity PL emission under these conditions revealed that the emitting groups were not influenced by acidity or basicity and the emission of CDs-NH2 under such conditions was mainly related to the hydrophobic phenyl groups derived from TPE precursor due to the formation of aggregates [20].

It should be noted that although multiple functional groups existed such as –OH, –COOH and –COOR as mentioned above, these groups may undergo different changes under different pH conditions, for example, protonation of amino, cationization of pyridinyl, anionization of hydroxyl, or even hydrolysis of COOR [26]. It is difficult to separately characterize the specific change of each group in detail. Moreover, most amino group in the precursor had been changed into pH-more-tolerant functional groups such as cyclized and aromatized structures during hydrothermal treatment, thus improving the stability of CDs while retaining the AIE feature. However, in the CDs-NH2 system, the solid-state emission mainly comes from the aggregation of CDs, wherein hydrophobic groups such as phenyl become motion-inactive so as to induce emission. It also well interpreted the generation of AIE behavior based on RIM mechanism.

Due to excellent photoluminescence and stability, the utilization of CDs-NH2 in cell imaging was evaluated. Human liver HepG2 cells were stained with CDs-NH2 (15 μg/mL) for 24 h in the DMEM medium in a 5% CO2 humidity incubator at 37 °C. Then, the cells were imaged by a confocal microscope after rinsing under laser excitation 405 nm. It can be seen that the morphologies and shapes of cells were well retained, and the blue emission from CDs-NH2 covered the cytoplasm with good imaging quality ( Fig. 4).

In summary, a general and straightforward method for sythesizing solid-emissive CDs was easily realized using AIE-active precursors via solvothermal treatment. TPE-based CDs showed typical AIE characteristic, better pH stability and bioimaging performance. This work helps us better understand the RIM mechanism accounting for AIE fluorescence and also provides a clue to the preparation of high-efficiency solid-emissive carbon nanomaterials. In future, more attention should be paid on the formation of AIE-active centers based on other AIE precursors so as to explore various types of AIE-active CDs with high luminescence efficiency and excellent luminescence performance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The submission (SYNMET-D-23–00440) has been received explicitly from all co-authors. And authors whose names appear on the submission have contributed sufficiently to the scientific work and therefore share collective responsibility and accountability for the results.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
The present manuscript is not under consideration for publication and has not been published elsewhere in any medium including electronic journals and computer databases of a public nature.

ce:section.ce:para: Shumei Xie: synthesis, optical spectra measurement and manuscript writing; Zhenxin Yue: literature survey and preliminary trial; Min Lin: cell imaging characterization; Guan Wang: XRD and XPS characterization and analysis; Ziqiang Xu: discussion on data analysis; Shimin Wang: discussion; Xinggui Gu: constructible discussion; Erjing Wang: project administration and manuscript revising.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52073082, 51603063, and 22073025) and Overseas Expertise Introduction Project for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:para: †Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: XRD, FT-IR, XPS, Raman, UV–vis absorption, PL spectra and particle size analysis of CDs-NH2 and TPE, and pH stability of TPE-NH2.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2023.117526.
